The shelter is finished. The armour is polished. Those facts do not interact with the argument unless they are simply further reminders that many things can be completed under irrelevant emotional or aesthetic conditions: people can hide crushes and still finish construction; soldiers can polish armour while music plays. So the scene already contains a warning about surface competence. Work can look orderly while the motives, selections, and surrounding signals are opaque.

Now take the epistemic premises as true. People excel at narrowing search. But their narrowing heuristics are not free-standing algorithms; their value may depend on the human judgments that give them meaning. So if an LLM inherits only the heuristic shape—what to attend to, which branches to prune—without inheriting the underlying judgments, then apparent efficiency may be hollow. It may move quickly through a space it does not actually understand how to organize.

Next, consider the Einstein prompt. If a system, given “why does light always travel at the same speed regardless of the observer,” arrives at Einstein-like structure, whether that is “creating knowledge” depends on how much of the result was already packed into the prompt. If the question already sharply constrains the conceptual destination, then the system may be doing sophisticated completion rather than discovery. In that case, apparent rediscovery is another example of polished armour: impressive finish, uncertain depth.

Then add the idea of compounding discoveries back into the draw pool. That can be described as self-improvement only if the discoveries are genuinely knowledge-bearing. But if some are false positives produced by narrowing heuristics detached from meaningful judgment, then feeding them back into future search does not merely preserve error; it gives error causal force. Earlier mistakes become priors. Search is no longer just imperfect; it becomes recursively contaminated. The system gets better at returning to the same attractive but brittle local minima.

So the decisive issue is not raw capacity to generate structured answers, nor even the visible acceleration gained by reusing past outputs. It is whether the system’s methods of narrowing are tethered to the kind of judgment that distinguishes a promising constraint from a loaded question and a true intermediate result from a seductive false positive.

In this world, completion and polish are cheap evidence. A shelter can be completed under concealment. Armour can gleam to music. An LLM can produce Einstein-like structure from a heavily suggestive prompt. None of these alone establishes that the right thing has happened for the right reason. And if the wrong things are recycled as if they were right, the whole process can become increasingly confident, increasingly efficient, and increasingly trapped.
